---
title: "When should a computer work?"
subtitle: "Electricity is not equally clean around the clock. Give a computation the freedom to pick its hour, and it can shed a quarter of its carbon — predictably, reliably, for free. A data story from the German power grid."
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go

from carbonwatch import viz
from carbonwatch.viz import ACCENT, CONFIG, CYAN, GRID, INK, INK2, LIME, MAGENTA, MUTED, ORANGE, REF_GRAY, SEQ

P = Path("../data/processed")
ENERGY = 20.0  # kWh: 4h x 5kW

day = pd.read_parquet(P / "example_day.parquet")["ci"].tz_convert("Europe/Berlin")
heat_de = pd.read_parquet(P / "heatmap_de.parquet")
daily = pd.read_parquet(P / "daily_windows.parquet")
mae = pd.read_parquet(P / "de_backtest_mae.parquet")
sim = pd.read_parquet(P / "de_sim_daily.parquet")
spatial = pd.read_parquet(P / "spatial_sim.parquet")

# headline numbers
vals = day.to_numpy()
kg = {s: float(vals[s : s + 4].mean() * ENERGY / 1000) for s in range(21)}
best_s, worst_s = min(kg, key=kg.get), max(kg, key=kg.get)
day_saving = 1 - kg[best_s] / kg[worst_s]

d_de = daily[daily.country == "DE"]
gap_med = d_de.gap.median()
midday_share = d_de.best_start.between(9, 14).mean()

totals = sim.sum()
save_fc = 1 - totals["forecast"] / totals["naive"]
save_or = 1 - totals["oracle"] / totals["naive"]
headroom = (totals["naive"] - totals["forecast"]) / (totals["naive"] - totals["oracle"])
kg_saved = totals["naive"] - totals["forecast"]

st = spatial.sum()
uk_share = (spatial.uk_oracle < spatial.de_oracle).mean()

skill = 1 - mae.loc["overall", "lgbm_exog"] / mae.loc["overall", "seasonal_naive"]
```

::: {.stat-row}
<div class="stat"><div class="stat-value">`{python} f"−{day_saving:.0%}".replace("%","")`%</div><div class="stat-label">CO₂ — same job, same day, better hour (14 Jul 2025)</div></div>
<div class="stat"><div class="stat-value">`{python} f"{headroom:.0%}".replace("%","")`%</div><div class="stat-label">of the theoretical saving captured by a forecast</div></div>
<div class="stat"><div class="stat-value">`{python} f"{kg_saved:.0f}"` kg</div><div class="stat-label">CO₂ avoided per job-year — ≈ `{python} f"{kg_saved/0.12:,.0f}"` km by car</div></div>
:::

## The grid breathes

On 14 July 2025, Germany's electricity was not one product but many. At six in
the evening, with solar fading and demand peaking, a kilowatt-hour carried
**`{python} f"{vals[worst_s:worst_s+4].mean():.0f}"` g of CO₂**. At noon, with
photovoltaics flooding the grid, the same kilowatt-hour carried
**`{python} f"{vals[best_s:best_s+4].mean():.0f}"` g** — `{python} f"{day_saving:.0%}"` less.

Most electricity users can't choose their hour. A computer often can. Training
runs, batch inference, data pipelines — they need hours of power, but rarely
*these particular* hours. Drag the job below and watch its footprint change.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "One 4-hour compute job (5 kW) on 14 July 2025, DE grid. Slider = start hour; emissions settle against actual carbon intensity."
hours = list(range(24))

def window(s):
    shapes = [dict(type="rect", x0=s - 0.5, x1=s + 3.5, y0=0, y1=1, yref="paper",
                   fillcolor="rgba(200,240,67,0.10)", line=dict(color=ACCENT, width=1))]
    annos = [dict(x=s + 1.5, y=1.06, yref="paper", showarrow=False,
                  text=f"<b>{kg[s]:.1f} kg CO₂</b>", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=13, color=ACCENT))]
    return shapes, annos

fig = go.Figure(go.Scatter(
    x=hours, y=vals, mode="lines", line=dict(color=LIME, width=2), name="carbon intensity",
    hovertemplate="%{x:02d}:00 · %{y:.0f} gCO₂/kWh<extra></extra>",
))
sh, an = window(17)
steps = []
for s in range(21):
    s_sh, s_an = window(s)
    steps.append(dict(method="relayout", label=f"{s:02d}", args=[{"shapes": s_sh, "annotations": s_an}]))
fig.update_layout(
    height=380, shapes=sh, annotations=an, showlegend=False,
    xaxis=dict(tickvals=[0, 6, 12, 18, 23], ticktext=["00:00", "06:00", "12:00", "18:00", "23:00"], title=""),
    yaxis=dict(title=dict(text="gCO₂/kWh", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=11))),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=17, steps=steps, currentvalue=dict(prefix="job starts at ", suffix=":00",
        font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=12, color=INK)), pad=dict(t=28),
        bgcolor=GRID, activebgcolor=MUTED, bordercolor=GRID, tickcolor=MUTED,
        font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=9, color=MUTED),
    )],
)
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

::: {.claim}
The cheapest climate action a computer can take is to look at the clock.
:::

## Not one lucky day

That July day is not an outlier. Across 2024–2025, the median German day offered
a **`{python} f"{gap_med:.0f}"` gCO₂/kWh gap** between its cleanest and
dirtiest four-hour window — the clean one landing between 09:00 and 14:00 on
`{python} f"{midday_share:.0%}"` of days, when solar output peaks. The pattern
is strongly seasonal: deep, reliable midday valleys in summer, flatter and
windier days in winter.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean carbon intensity of German electricity by hour and month, 2024–2025. Computed from ENTSO-E generation data."
months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
scale = [[i / (len(SEQ) - 1), c] for i, c in enumerate(SEQ)]
fig = go.Figure(go.Heatmap(
    z=heat_de.T.to_numpy(), x=hours, y=months, colorscale=scale,
    colorbar=dict(title=dict(text="gCO₂/kWh", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=10)),
                  tickfont=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=10, color=MUTED), thickness=10, outlinewidth=0),
    hovertemplate="%{y} · %{x:02d}:00 · %{z:.0f} gCO₂/kWh<extra></extra>", xgap=1, ygap=1,
))
fig.update_layout(height=420, xaxis=dict(tickvals=[0, 6, 12, 18, 23],
                  ticktext=["00:00", "06:00", "12:00", "18:00", "23:00"]), yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"))
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

## Can you see it coming?

A schedule is only as good as its forecast: at midnight you must commit
tomorrow's plan. We trained a gradient-boosted model on 2024 to predict hourly
carbon intensity up to 36 hours ahead, then tested it on all of 2025 — never
letting it see information that wasn't public at forecast time.

The decisive ingredient is not the model. It is Germany's **day-ahead wind and
solar forecasts**, published daily by grid operators. With them, mid-range error
drops by more than half; the carbon intensity of a solar-heavy grid is, to a
large degree, a weather forecast wearing an emissions factor.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean absolute error of day-ahead CI forecasts by horizon, test year 2025. Lower is better."
buckets = ["1-6h", "7-12h", "13-24h", "25-36h"]
models = [("persistence", "Persistence", REF_GRAY), ("seasonal_naive", "Seasonal naïve", ORANGE),
          ("lgbm", "LightGBM", CYAN), ("lgbm_exog", "LightGBM + wind/solar forecast", LIME)]
fig = go.Figure()
for col, label, color in models:
    fig.add_bar(x=buckets, y=mae.loc[buckets, col], name=label, marker=dict(color=color),
                hovertemplate=label + " · %{x} · MAE %{y:.0f} g/kWh<extra></extra>")
fig.update_layout(height=380, barmode="group", bargap=0.25, bargroupgap=0.08,
                  yaxis=dict(title=dict(text="MAE, gCO₂/kWh", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=11))),
                  xaxis=dict(title=dict(text="forecast horizon", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=11))))
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

Overall the model beats a seasonal-naïve baseline by
**`{python} f"{skill:.0%}"`** — and in the window that matters for scheduling
(the next 24 hours), its error is `{python} f"{mae.loc['13-24h','lgbm_exog']:.0f}"` g/kWh
against the baseline's `{python} f"{mae.loc['13-24h','seasonal_naive']:.0f}"`.

## The experiment

So: one four-hour training job at five kilowatts, every day of 2025, free to
start at any hour. Three schedulers compete. One picks a random hour — the
computer that doesn't look at the clock. One follows our forecast, committed the
night before. One cheats: perfect knowledge of the future, the physical ceiling.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Total 2025 emissions of the daily 4-hour job under three scheduling policies, DE grid."
bars = [("Random start", totals["naive"], REF_GRAY, ""),
        ("Forecast picks the hour", totals["forecast"], LIME, f"−{save_fc:.0%}"),
        ("Perfect foresight", totals["oracle"], "rgba(0,0,0,0)", f"−{save_or:.0%}")]
fig = go.Figure()
for label, val, color, delta in bars:
    outline = dict(color=LIME, width=1.5) if color == "rgba(0,0,0,0)" else dict(width=0)
    fig.add_bar(y=[label], x=[val], orientation="h", marker=dict(color=color, line=outline),
                text=f"<b>{val:,.0f} kg</b>  {delta}", textposition="outside",
                textfont=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=12, color=INK),
                hovertemplate=label + " · %{x:,.0f} kg CO₂<extra></extra>", showlegend=False)
fig.update_layout(height=260, xaxis=dict(range=[0, totals["naive"] * 1.35], title=""),
                  yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"), bargap=0.45)
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

The forecast scheduler cut emissions by **`{python} f"{save_fc:.0%}"`**. The
omniscient one managed `{python} f"{save_or:.0%}"`. In other words, an ordinary
public-data forecast captures **`{python} f"{headroom:.0%}"` of the theoretical
maximum** — on a solar-shaped grid, tomorrow's clean window is simply not that
hard to see coming. Per job that is `{python} f"{kg_saved:.0f}"` kg of CO₂ a
year, the footprint of driving `{python} f"{kg_saved/0.12:,.0f}"` km. Across a
fleet of a thousand such jobs: `{python} f"{kg_saved:.0f}"` tonnes.

## Or just move?

Timing is not the only degree of freedom — batch compute can also change
country. Great Britain's grid, gas-and-wind shaped and free of lignite, ran
cleaner than Germany's on `{python} f"{uk_share:.0%}"` of 2025 days.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean carbon intensity by month, 2024–2025: Germany vs Great Britain."
m_de = daily[daily.country == "DE"].set_index("day").mean_ci
m_uk = daily[daily.country == "UK"].set_index("day").mean_ci
fig = go.Figure()
for name, s, color in [("Germany", m_de, LIME), ("Great Britain", m_uk, CYAN)]:
    ms = s.groupby(pd.to_datetime(s.index).to_period("M")).mean()
    fig.add_scatter(x=ms.index.to_timestamp(), y=ms, mode="lines", name=name,
                    line=dict(color=color, width=2),
                    hovertemplate=name + " · %{x|%b %Y} · %{y:.0f} g/kWh<extra></extra>")
fig.update_layout(height=340, yaxis=dict(title=dict(text="gCO₂/kWh", font=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=11)),
                  rangemode="tozero"))
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "The same daily job, 2025 total: staying and timing vs moving. Green = Germany, blue = Great Britain."
opts = [("Stay DE · random hour", st.de_naive, LIME, 0.45),
        ("Stay DE · best hour", st.de_oracle, LIME, 1.0),
        ("Move UK · random hour", st.uk_naive, CYAN, 0.45),
        ("Move UK · best hour", st.uk_oracle, CYAN, 1.0)]
fig = go.Figure()
for label, val, color, op in opts:
    fig.add_bar(y=[label], x=[val], orientation="h", marker=dict(color=color, opacity=op),
                text=f"<b>{val:,.0f} kg</b>  −{1 - val / st.de_naive:.0%}" if val < st.de_naive else f"<b>{val:,.0f} kg</b>",
                textposition="outside", textfont=dict(family=viz.MONO, size=12, color=INK),
                hovertemplate=label + " · %{x:,.0f} kg CO₂<extra></extra>", showlegend=False)
fig.update_layout(height=300, xaxis=dict(range=[0, st.de_naive * 1.4]), yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"), bargap=0.4)
fig.show(config=CONFIG)
```

Moving beats waiting: relocating the job to Britain saves more than perfect
timing in Germany ever could. But the comparison is not symmetric. Changing
*when* a job runs costs nothing — no data transfer, no residency questions, no
second data centre. Changing *where* is an infrastructure decision. The honest
reading: **timing is the free lever, geography is the big one**, and they
compound — a timed job in Britain emits `{python} f"{1 - st.uk_oracle / st.de_naive:.0%}"`
less than a random one in Germany.

## What this doesn't fix

Carbon-aware scheduling optimises *when*, not *how much*. The job still burns
the same kilowatt-hours; the grid still burns what it burns. Three honest
limits:

- **Average, not marginal.** We price each hour at the grid's average intensity.
  The *marginal* plant answering one extra kilowatt is often gas regardless of
  hour, so system-level savings from small shifts are smaller than average-based
  accounting suggests. At fleet scale, though, demand that reliably chases clean
  hours changes what gets built — the long-run signal points the same way.
- **Production, not consumption.** Our intensity ignores imports and exports;
  German midday solar partly flows abroad, and winter imports flow in.
- **Timing is a complement, not an alibi.** The real decarbonisation is the
  grid's own. Flexible demand just stops wasting the clean hours the grid
  already produces — a lever that is real, measurable, and free, but a lever,
  not an engine.

## Methods

Carbon intensity is computed from [ENTSO-E](https://transparency.entsoe.eu/)
actual generation per production type for bidding zone DE-LU (15-minute
resolution, 2024–2025), weighted by IPCC lifecycle emission factors per fuel,
resampled hourly. The series correlates at **r = 0.992** with the independent
[Energy-Charts](https://energy-charts.info) (Fraunhofer ISE) reference; our
conservative lignite factor gives a −74 g/kWh level offset that does not affect
timing decisions. British data comes from the
[NESO Carbon Intensity API](https://carbonintensity.org.uk/).

The forecaster is a LightGBM model over lagged CI, calendar features, and
ENTSO-E day-ahead wind, solar, and load forecasts, trained on 2024, tested
out-of-sample on 2025 from daily midnight origins, horizons 1–36 h. Day-ahead
exogenous forecasts are only used for horizons where they were genuinely
published. The scheduling simulation settles every policy against actual
intensity. The job spec (4 h at 5 kW ≈ one 8-GPU node) is a scale anchor —
percentages are independent of it.

Code, tests, and reproduction steps: **[github repository]** — every figure on
this page rebuilds from public APIs with two commands.

::: {.small-note}
Data: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform · NESO Carbon Intensity API · Energy-Charts
(Fraunhofer ISE). Analysis and site: Python, LightGBM, Plotly, Quarto.
July 2026.
:::
